column1:
Which CNCF project(s) do you primarily contribute to?
Specify the project(s) where you are most active (e.g., Kubernetes, Prometheus, Argo).
column2:
What is your primary contributor role?
Select the role that best describes your core activities within the community.
column3:
How long have you been contributing to CNCF projects?
This helps us understand if AI adoption patterns differ between seasoned maintainers and new contributors.
column4:
Do any of the projects you contribute to currently disclose the usage of AI tools in its documentation or governance?
Does the project's README, website, or contributing guide explicitly mention if AI is used in the development process?
column5:
Are you aware of any specific AI policies governing your project(s)?
Whether at the project or org level, are there rules you are expected to follow?
column6:
If yes, which policy does your project have?
Please describe the current "vibe" or official stance on AI tools in your project(s).
column7: If yes, could you provide link to the repository/policy?
column8:
How do you currently use AI in your workflow? Select all that apply.
Please categorize your level of AI interaction from simple external queries to fully integrated automation.
column9: Which AI tools do you use most frequently for project work? Tell us your "stack" (e.g., GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, KubeGPT, etc.).
column10: Where is your projects integrating AI-powered tools? Identify where AI is being used as a project-wide utility rather than just an individual's personal tool.
column11: In your opinion, in which areas has AI been most helpful? (Select all that apply) Where are you seeing the biggest "productivity wins" or quality improvements?
column12: Do you believe AI-assisted contributions should require disclosure in pull requests? We are seeking community sentiment on whether "AI-authored" tags should be a standard requirement for transparency.
column13:
When reviewing pull requests from new contributors in your CNCF project, what challenges or concerns do you encounter if the submitted code appears to be partially or fully generated using AI tools?
As a reviewer, how do you handle the influx of code that may not have been written by a human?
column14:
What is your biggest concern regarding AI usage in CNCF projects?
From legal liability to the "noise" of low-quality PRs, what keeps you up at night?
column15: Anything else you’d like to share on the topic? Use this space for any nuances we might have missed in the multiple-choice questions.

column15: I think any policies we add should be on quality, not on AI usage. You can use AI and do a perfect, bug free PR, and you can use AI and do complete slop. The difference is the effort in making the PR. AI surely multiplies the noise as it lowers the barrier to file a PR, but we should have a clear policy on expectations that PRs need to be high quality (regardless of who did it) or they get closed. Most of our problems are related to low quality and volume coming from users who are trying to get an LFX mentorship anyway.

column15: While contributing to Jaeger, I’ve observed that AI-assisted tools can be highly effective for tasks like code exploration, refactoring, and onboarding. However, challenges remain around trust, consistency, and security of generated outputs. Establishing standardized guidelines for evaluation, human-in-the-loop validation, and secure usage will be key to enabling broader and more confident adoption across CNCF projects.

column15:
It's mentioned in the policy doc I linked but just to make sure it's visible: OpenTofu's prohibition on using generative AI is primarily due to its heritage as a fork of another codebase whose license was changed, where the owners of that old codebase have previously attempted to sue based on similarity of code contributed to OpenTofu.

The maintainers avoid looking at the original project's source code to avoid risk of "taint" and so cannot effectively verify whether submitted code is similar to Terraform, but anecdotal reports suggest that current coding agent technologies have access to information about what OpenTofu is a fork of and will autonomously fetch Terraform source code for analysis before making changes to OpenTofu. Therefore we have chosen to minimize risk of further timewasting lawsuits by prohibiting use of this technology against the main OpenTofu codebase.

column13: If any PR is without prior discussion via an issue, it is either left stale till someone opens an issue and discusses what the PR is and why. If an issue was made beforehand it goes through the normal process. This was done not to reject AI PRs but to make sure there is a human behind the PR and not just an automated bot.

column15:
I think accountability is another major concern. Specially in an open source environment. If a PR is merged into a repo and causes some vulnerabilities or errors, the main concern is to find and solve that issue. It is precisely the 'finding' part which is so difficult in AI PR's and even more so when there is no one prompting.
Someone is to be held accountable, not for merging a wrong/vuln PR but for being able to solve and find the issue. A maintainer who merges a PR, and a contributor who contributed are accountable for that. With AI PR's and AI Reviews, no one is. Not even the AI remembers what it wrote.

column15: When handling AI-assisted PRs, the biggest challenge is determining whether the PR submitter truly understands the problem being addressed. In the HAMi community, we've encountered numerous cases where submitters relied on AI analysis to understand the project and the issue, but they fundamentally misunderstood the project's principles and the problem from the outset, resulting in invalid PRs.

column15:
I think LLMs are a good tool - but the human is still the quality gate and too many humans are disregarding that; on both sides of the discussion. Yes we get a lot of slop from AI "enthusiasts" but we also get a lot of valuable help from LLMs and people using them. 

At the end of the day the operator of the LLM has to be critical of the result of the LLM and determine whether that result is correct.

column15: I think it's difficult to avoid AI contributions. We have to accept it as a tool and that more and more people will rely on it. I think disclosure is a must and part of responsible use. If maintainers/triagers eventually start to be overloaded with AI contributions done irresponsibly or in an automated fashion, we should have a streamlined system to warn users and consider temporary suspension from interaction (a temporary block) in the org's projects. Many open source projects are going this route.

column15: AI is a useful productivity tool for learning, debugging, documentation, and understanding large codebases. However, contributors should remain responsible for the code they submit and be able to explain, maintain, and improve it during code review. In my experience as a newer CNCF contributor, AI is most valuable when used as an assistant rather than a replacement for understanding the project and its development practices.
